observationFocus: Ignore top-page UI such as level selectors, buttons, login text, and menu icons; focus only on the puzzle board.
Focus on the player, boxes, targets, walls, blocked paths, and whether a push creates or avoids deadlocks.
Treat the pale beige floor as walkable space and the dark stone pattern as solid wall / non-walkable area.
Treat the pink dots as targets, the orange or brown crates as boxes, and the human sprite as the player.
Before proposing a move, verify the immediate next tile in that direction: if it is a wall or blocked box chain, do not choose that move.
When the board is static and legible, prefer a bounded plan of 2-4 grounded moves instead of a single vague nudge.
Explain progress in concrete puzzle terms: player position, box access, target alignment, and whether a path just opened or closed.
Describe the puzzle layout and movement consequences instead of generic scene narration.
notes: You can only push boxes, never pull them.
Avoid pushing boxes into unrecoverable corners.
If the very next tile in the chosen direction is a wall, that move is invalid and should not be selected.
Prefer moves that create visible progress or better positioning over moves that simply bump into walls.
When a short sequence is clearly available, choose it and keep each step grounded in the current board layout.

actions.label: Up
actions.description: Move the player up once, pushing a box only if the path allows it.

actions.label: Left
actions.description: Move the player left once, pushing a box only if the path allows it.

actions.label: Right
actions.description: Move the player right once, pushing a box only if the path allows it.

actions.label: Down
actions.description: Move the player down once, pushing a box only if the path allows it.
